name: title-card-cinematic
description: Full-screen cinematic title card for 1920x1080. Hero title with optional subtitle, thin horizontal rule divider, dark background solid, subtle vignette via radial gradient overlay, and Fractal Noise grain on an adjustment layer. Use for documentary titles, film intros, brand identity stings, chapter cards, and any content that needs cinematic gravitas.

design_notes.palette: Cinematic dark palette §3.1. Background #0B0B0F (near-black with cool cast), primary text #F9FAFB (warm white), subtitle #94A3B8 (muted cool gray). Film-appropriate — avoids pure black and pure white per §3.1 dark theme guidance.
design_notes.easing: easeOutCubic (0.215, 0.61, 0.355, 1) for scale/opacity entrance — the 'Cubic Family: most used by professionals' per §1.1. Duration 1.0–1.2s for hero elements = 'Cinematic reveal: 500–1200ms' from §1.2 duration table. Subtitle enters 0.2s after hero title.
design_notes.scale: Hero title enters at scale 105%, animates to 100%. Per §1.3 Principle 1 Squash & Stretch: 'Scale deviation of 5–10% (90–110% range) — Subtle (logos, UI).' This 5% push-in is the cinematic slow-zoom reveal signature.
design_notes.grain: Fractal Noise on adjustment layer per §2.4 recipe. Turbulent type, Soft Linear noise, Contrast 150, opacity 4–8% on adjustment layer set to Overlay blend mode. Barely perceptible but removes the 'digital clean' look.
design_notes.vignette: Radial gradient oval mask on dark solid, Feather 300px, blend mode Multiply at 60% opacity. Per §2.6 color grading workflow: 'Apply Vignette: create oval mask on black solid, feather 200–400px, set to Multiply'.
design_notes.timing: Total 6s: entrance 0–1.2s, hold 1.2–5.0s (3.8s for reading), exit 5.0–6.0s (1s fade). Per §1.5 architecture: '20–30% entrance, 35–45% core, 10–15% outro' — at 6s: entrance ~1.2s (20%), hold ~3.8s (63%), exit ~1.0s (17%).

hero_title.description: Main hero title text. All-caps recommended. Scale and font are designed for 1–3 words.

subtitle_text.description: Subtitle text beneath the rule divider. Sentence case. Can be left empty for title-only variant.

background_color.description: RGB 0–1 range. Default #0B0B0F near-black. Adjust for different mood — slightly warmer [0.059, 0.047, 0.043] for a film-noir feel.

hero_font_size.description: Hero title font size in px. Research §3.3 type scale: Hero/main title 80–160px. Default 120px is mid-range for wide titles.

accent_color.description: Color of the thin horizontal rule. Default warm white #F9FAFB. Change to a brand color for a subtle accent.

grain_opacity.description: Opacity of the grain adjustment layer (0–100). 4–8% is barely perceptible but removes the clinical digital look. Increase for a more stylized film grain effect.

steps.note: Background #0B0B0F = [0.043, 0.043, 0.059]. Per §3.1: 'Near-black with cool blue cast' — the cinematic dark foundation. Never pure black: the subtle cool cast makes brighter elements appear more luminous by contrast.

Dark background solid at #0B0B0F. A dedicated solid layer (rather than relying on comp background) allows effects like gradient overlays or color corrections to be applied non-destructively on top of it.

Pure black solid that will become the vignette via an oval mask with heavy feather. Per §2.6 cinematic workflow: 'Apply Vignette: create oval mask on black solid, feather 200–400px, set to Multiply.' Vignette darkens edges and draws focus to center — essential cinematic depth technique.

Inverted ellipse mask on the vignette solid. The ellipse covers most of the frame; inverting it means the EDGES of the solid remain visible (as the vignette), while the center is masked out (transparent). Feather 300px creates an ultra-soft gradient from center to edges. The result: dark corners, bright center.

Vignette solid set to Multiply blend mode. Per §2.6: 'Multiply: always darkens. Shadows on white, overlaying dirt/texture.' At Multiply mode, black stays black (maximum darkening), while any transparent areas show through. Combined with the inverted oval mask, this creates edge darkening without affecting the center area.
Vignette at 60% opacity — visible but not crushing the blacks. At 100% the corners would go pure black and feel heavy. 60% creates the cinematic edge-fall-off without completely darkening corners.

Hero title at 120pt ExtraBold. Per §3.3 type scale: 'Hero/main title: 80–160px, Black/ExtraBold — full-screen statement.' Color #F9FAFB = [0.976, 0.980, 0.984] — warm white that is easier on the eyes than pure white. Tracking +150: per §3.3 rule 'All-caps titles (any size): +100 to +200 — All-caps always requires aggressive tracking.' THE JOURNEY in all-caps at 120px with +150 tracking reads as cinematic and authoritative.

Hero title centered at x=960 (frame center) and y=490 (slightly above center). Placing the title slightly above the mathematical center (540) follows the optical illusion principle: the eye perceives geometrically-centered elements as slightly below center. Positioning at y=490 creates perceived visual centering. The subtitle will sit below, pushing the overall block to a visually balanced position.

Thin horizontal rule between title and subtitle. This is a classic cinematic title card technique — a single thin line creates visual separation and rhythm between the hero and supporting text. The rule gives the eye a 'beat' to rest on.
Shape group for the horizontal rule rectangle.

Thin rule: 240px wide × 1px tall. Width 240px = 1/8th of frame width, creating a subtle visual marker rather than a full-width divider. This proportion is cinematic — a full-width rule would be too corporate/UI, while a narrow rule maintains elegant restraint.

Rule color #F9FAFB at 60% opacity — slightly muted so it doesn't compete with the title text above it. This is a secondary visual element (§1.3 Principle 7: Secondary action). Full opacity would give it too much weight.

Rule positioned at y=576 — approximately 86px below the hero title baseline (at y=490 + ~86px for 120pt text body). This 86px gap creates proper breathing room between title and rule, maintaining the leading ratio of ~1.15× for display type (§3.3).

Subtitle at 28pt Light. Per §3.3 type scale: 'Subtitle/secondary: 36–54px, Medium/SemiBold' — using 28pt Light creates more contrast against the 120pt ExtraBold title. Per §3.3 pairing rules: 'Weight contrast rule: Title should be at minimum 2 weights heavier than body copy.' ExtraBold (8/8) vs Light (2/8) = 6 weights of contrast. Color #94A3B8 = [0.580, 0.639, 0.722] — muted cool gray, subordinate to title. Tracking +120 for small-size display text with open feel per §3.3.

Subtitle at y=608, 32px below the rule divider at y=576. This 32px gap between rule and subtitle gives the rule space to breathe as a visual pause. The total title block (hero title + rule + subtitle) spans roughly y=490–635, centering this cluster near y=562 — close to optical center (y=540) with slight upward bias.

Adjustment layer for film grain effect. Adjustment layers apply effects to all layers below them in the stack. Placing this at the top of the stack means the grain composites over everything — background, vignette, title, and rule — creating a unified filmic texture across the entire frame.

Apply Fractal Noise to the grain adjustment layer. Per §2.4 Fractal Noise technique: 'Turbulent type, Soft Linear noise — organic textures.' This will be configured to create film grain. Effect Match Name 'ADBE Fractal Noise' is the native AE name for Fractal Noise (Effect > Noise & Grain > Fractal Noise).

Fractal Type = Turbulent (value 1). Per §2.4: 'Turbulent: More jagged, energetic — Fire, water, organic texture.' Turbulent fractal type produces grain that reads as authentic film grain rather than digital noise.
Contrast = 400. High contrast on the noise makes grain particles distinct — this is essential so that at very low opacity the grain is still perceptible as texture rather than a uniform gray wash.

Brightness = -60. Per §2.4 recipe: 'Brightness: -30 to -60 (darken for richer look).' Darkening the noise makes it appear as dark grain particles on the image, matching the look of real film grain which manifests as silver halide clumping in shadow areas.

Scale = 25. Small scale creates tight, fine-grain texture rather than large blotches. Film grain typically appears at a very fine scale. Per §2.4: 'Scale: 200–400 for large features' — we want the opposite for grain: very small scale = fine grain.

params.propertyPath: ADBE Effect Parade/ADBE Fractal Noise/ADBE Fractal Noise-0014

steps.note: Expression on Fractal Noise Evolution property: 'time * 100'. Per §2.4 recipe: 'Evolution: time * 100 (expression for animation).' This continuously evolves the grain pattern each frame, creating the film grain flutter effect. At 29.97fps, the evolution advances ~3 units per frame, producing organic frame-to-frame variation.

Grain layer set to Overlay blend mode. Per §2.6: 'Overlay: Screen on light areas, Multiply on dark. Texture overlay, contrast enhancement.' Overlay mode makes the grain interact naturally with both the dark background and the bright text — it brightens highlights where grain is light and darkens shadows where grain is dark, replicating real film emulsion behavior.

Grain layer at 6% opacity. Per §2.6 texture workflow: 'Overlay at 40–60% opacity adds grain/texture naturally' — but film grain in motion graphics should be far more subtle than texture on footage. 4–8% places the grain at the threshold of perception — felt more than seen, removing the synthetic digital look.

Hero title opacity: 0 → 100 over 1.0s. Per §1.2 duration table: 'Cinematic reveal: 500–1200ms' — 1.0s is in the cinematic range. Combined with the scale animation (next step), this is the signature cinematic 'emerge' entrance where the title materializes with weight.

Hero title scale: 105% → 100% over 1.0s (concurrently with opacity). The 5% scale push-in creates the cinematic slow-zoom effect — as though the camera is very slowly zooming out while the title materializes. Per §1.3 Principle 1: 'Subtle (logos, UI): Scale deviation of 5–10% (90–110% range).' Combined with opacity fade-in, this is one of the most refined cinematic title entrance techniques.

easeOutCubic behavior on hero title opacity. Per §1.1 Cubic Family: 'easeOutCubic (0.215, 0.61, 0.355, 1) — strong curves, most used by professionals.' The ease_out type maps to this curve. For cinematic slow reveals, a cubic ease-out gives a natural, weighted deceleration — not as snappy as Expo, which would feel too commercial.

easeOut on hero title scale. The scale animation from 105% to 100% uses ease_out so it starts at a perceptible zoom rate and decelerates smoothly to rest at 100% — the zoom-out slows to a stop. This mirrors real camera motion physics.

Rule divider scales in horizontally (X only). Scale X: 0 → 100 while Y stays at 100 — creates a horizontal wipe/draw-on effect. Starts 0.9s (before hero title finishes at 1.0s) and completes at 1.4s. Begins during the final 10% of the title entrance, per §1.3 Principle 7: 'Secondary action STARTS when primary action ENDS (or within the last 20% of the primary action).' The rule starts to appear just before the title fully lands, creating seamless flow.
easeOut on rule scale. The horizontal draw-on effect uses ease_out for a natural line-drawing motion that decelerates at the endpoints.

Subtitle opacity: 0 → 100 starting at t=1.2s (0.2s after hero title finishes at t=1.0). Per the brief: 'subtitle enters 0.2s after with slide-up.' Duration 0.6s for subtitle vs 1.0s for hero — subtitle is a smaller element, faster animation per §1.3 Principle 8: 'Fast elements read as light/energetic; slow elements read as heavy/important.' The subtitle is lighter, so it animates faster.

Subtitle slide-up: position from [960, 628] to [960, 608] — 20px upward movement concurrent with opacity fade-in. This subtle 20px slide-up complements the opacity reveal, creating a 'rises into place' feel. Per §1.3 Principle 5 Arcs: even a 10–20px vertical deviation creates organic feel. The slide-up matches the title's 105%→100% scale (which also has a slight downward pull as it scales in from above-center).
easeOut on subtitle opacity. Consistent ease_out family for all entrance animations in this comp.
easeOut on subtitle slide-up. The 20px rise decelerates naturally to rest.

Hero title exit: fade to opacity 0 over 1.0s starting at t=5.0. Slow fade-out matches the slow fade-in for cinematic symmetry. Per §1.2: 'Text exit (disappear): 6–12 frames = 250–500ms (faster than entrance)' — at 29.97fps, 1.0s is actually on the longer side, appropriate for a cinematic title that deserves a slow, graceful exit. The full comp is 6s, so the 1.0s exit occupies the final 16.7% — matching §1.5 architecture 'outro 10–15%'.

Rule divider exit: fades out at t=5.0–5.8 (0.8s). Slightly faster than the hero title exit (1.0s) so the supporting element disappears before the hero — reinforcing the visual hierarchy: the last thing we see is the hero title.

Subtitle exit: fades out at t=5.0–5.6 (0.6s). The fastest exit — subtitle entered last and exits first. This reverse-stagger on exit (subtitle, rule, title) mirrors the entrance in reverse, creating bookended choreography: title first in, title last out.

easeInOut on hero title exit opacity. For a slow cinematic fade-out, ease_in_out (symmetric S-curve) is appropriate — the exit starts gently (no jarring pop-off) and accelerates through the middle before completing. This is the 'Cinematic Slow / Languid' approach from §1.1: 'easeInOutSine (0.445, 0.05, 0.55, 0.95) — very gentle, film-like'.
easeInOut on rule exit. Consistent cinematic exit easing for all elements.
easeInOut on subtitle exit. All exits use ease_in_out to maintain the cinematic slow-dissolve character of the piece.
